In a previous session I did highlight a few experiments I would like to try on the moe block. Here are the summarize information fromt hat conversation with the agent: 
MoEBlock for TransformerEngine JAX — Handoff Summary
Goal
Build a self-contained Flax Linen MoEBlock in transformer_engine/jax/flax/moe.py that decouples MoE optimization from Maxtext's moe.py so we can experiment with weight layouts, sharding strategies, and communication ordering without touching Maxtext. Initial scope: ring-of-experts EP only; later extensible to NCCL EP / ragged-A2A.

What landed
transformer_engine/jax/flax/moe.py — MoEBlock(TransformerEngineBase)
End-to-end MoE forward: gate → fused router (with optional aux loss, expert bias, sigmoid/softmax score, group-topk for DeepSeek) → token dispatch → grouped GEMM FFN (SwiGLU-style: act(wi_0) * wi_1 → wo) → token combine. Ring-EP wraps a shared _forward_body in jax.shard_map that all-gathers inputs/logits before and psum_scatters outputs after. Gate runs outside the shard_map so each EP shard projects only its local tokens and we all_gather the smaller logits, not full inputs.

Two pluggable permutation backends (selected by permutation_backend):

"pure_jax" (default, faster in profiling): unfused_token_dispatch / unfused_token_combine (argsort-based, custom-VJP for the sort).
"triton": TE's token_dispatch / token_combine (fused Triton kernels with built-in tight alignment padding).
Both backends were unified in transformer_engine/jax/permutation.py (the old mt_permutation.py was merged in and renamed to drop Maxtext coupling).

tests/jax/test_moe_block.py — single-device suite
Forward/shape/finite, backward grad, pure_jax↔triton equivalence, aux-loss returned, group-topk DeepSeek, JIT determinism. Uses an _unwrap_partitioned helper to strip Flax LogicallyPartitioned wrappers before jnp.isfinite/jnp.allclose. aux_loss path casts logits and aux scores to float32 to avoid BF16 overflow → NaN.

tests/jax/test_distributed_moe_block.py — EP=2 × FSDP=2 suite
2×2 mesh ("ep", "fsdp"), logical rules ("exp","ep"), ("batch","fsdp"), ("embed","fsdp"), input_axes=("batch", None, None), asserts physical sharding specs on weights and numerical match against a single-device oracle for forward / aux loss / gradients. Skips when fewer than 4 devices.

Sharding architecture (3 layers, only one is a separate paradigm)
Per-op custom_partitioning rules registered on each TE primitive (dense, grouped_dense, token_dispatch, fused_topk_*). Handles DP/TP/FSDP automatically given the input shardings — no shard_map required.
Declaring shardings via nn.with_logical_partitioning(init, axes) on params + TE's with_sharding_constraint_by_logical_axes on activations. This is not a third paradigm — it's just a logical-axis naming layer over NamedSharding + with_sharding_constraint, which the XLA GSPMD partitioner consumes.
jax.shard_map — the only true second paradigm. Used only in _forward_ring_ep because ring-EP collectives (all_gather inputs/logits → route → roll → FFN → psum_scatter) span multiple primitives, use axis_index for the per-shard expert roll, and pass data-dependent token counts between primitives — none of which custom_partitioning can express.
A future ragged_all_to_all EP path also needs shard_map for the same three reasons (cross-primitive collective, axis_index source/dest indexing, data-dependent A2A sizes from the local router). Replacing shard_map would require fusing the whole MoE pipeline into one custom primitive — much bigger redesign.

Open follow-ups / known issues
pure_jax + align_size > 0 is incompatible with TE grouped_dense. Maxtext's pure-JAX path is unpadded; their padded path uses TE Triton dispatch which produces a tight buffer (M == sum(group_sizes)). PR #36's loose static-buffer trick (M = N + E*(A-1), overflow into a sink expert) violates gemm.cpp:1029's m == sum_group_sizes assertion. We do not want to fold overflow into the last expert (load imbalance), and we do not want to relax the C++ check (the kernel relies on it). The cleanest fix is to raise ValueError at MoEBlock construction when permutation_backend == "pure_jax" and align_size > 0 until we expose a non-TE GMM backend that tolerates loose buffers. Currently test_align_size_equivalence_pure_jax is xfail.
Helper audit. Activation sharding goes through with_sharding_constraint_by_logical_axes (TE wrapper). Param init uses Flax's with_logical_partitioning — there's no TE equivalent and the two compose cleanly via flax.linen.logical_axis_rules. Ring-EP collectives use raw jax.lax.all_gather/psum_scatter because TE's lax_paral_op family is keyed on MeshResource axes (no ep_resource field).
The two experiments this block was designed to enable
Experiment 1 — Quantize before FSDP all-gather
Today: grouped_dense (and dense) all-gather BF16 weights along the FSDP axis, then quantize the full gathered tensor to FP8 on every device. This wastes AG bandwidth (BF16 instead of FP8) and replicates the quantization cost across all FSDP shards.

Goal: quantize each shard's weight slice locally (FP8 quant only on 1/num_fsdp of the tensor), then all-gather the FP8 + scales. Wins: AG bandwidth ÷ 2 (FP8 vs BF16), weight-quant time ÷ num_fsdp.

Mechanism in TE today: dense accepts kernel_fsdp_info=(mesh_axis_name, dim_index). (None, -1) (the current default in grouped_dense) means "no manual FSDP handling — trust XLA to AG implicitly before quantize". grouped_dense currently rejects any non-default value. Path forward: thread kernel_fsdp_info through grouped_dense, perform the quantize on the local shard, then do the AG on the ScaledTensor (data + scales) using flatten_axis consistent with the kernel layout.

Where in MoEBlock: all three weight tensors (wi_0, wi_1, wo) flow through grouped_dense calls in _dispatch_and_expert_ffn / the FFN body. Adding a quantize_before_fsdp_ag: bool knob (default False to preserve current behavior) and propagating kernel_fsdp_info from MoEBlock (which knows the FSDP mesh axis from logical rules) to those grouped_dense calls is the change.

Experiment 2 — Eliminate loop_transpose_fusion overhead by choosing weight layout & FSDP-shard axis
Today: weights are shaped wi_0/wi_1: (E, H, I) with logical axes ("exp", "embed", "mlp") and wo: (E, I, H) with ("exp", "mlp", "embed"). The FSDP all-gather output layout doesn't always match the layout grouped_dense expects on its contracting/non-contracting dims, so XLA inserts an implicit transpose fused into the AG loop — that's the loop_transpose_fusion overhead seen in profiles.

Goal: make both the weight-axis permutation and which weight axis is FSDP-sharded user-configurable (mirroring Maxtext's flexibility), so a profiling sweep can pick the combination that lets AG output land in the layout grouped_dense already wants → no transpose, no fusion overhead.

Mechanism in MoEBlock: the existing wi_kernel_axes / wo_kernel_axes constructor args already let the user reshape the logical layout (e.g., switch ("exp","embed","mlp") ↔ ("exp","mlp","embed")); combined with the rules table, the user already chooses which axis hits fsdp. What's missing is (a) confirming grouped_dense's custom_partitioning cooperates with all permutations (it currently assumes E is the leading dim, which is fine), and (b) corresponding wi_0.shape / contraction-axis arguments to grouped_dense need to follow the logical permutation. The block intentionally keeps both axes pluggable so this experiment is a config sweep, not a rewrite. The user wants to discuss the API design for exposing layout to users before implementing this — flagged as needing a discussion before code.

Files of interest for the next agent
transformer_engine/jax/flax/moe.py — MoEBlock (~770 lines).
transformer_engine/jax/permutation.py — both backends, custom-VJP sort.
transformer_engine/jax/dense.py — grouped_dense, kernel_fsdp_info plumbing for Experiment 1.
transformer_engine/jax/sharding.py — MeshResource, with_sharding_constraint_by_logical_axes, helper utilities.
transformer_engine/jax/csrc/extensions/gemm.cpp:1029 — strict m == sum(group_sizes) assertion (do not relax).
tests/jax/test_moe_block.py, tests/jax/test_distributed_moe_block.py — single-device + EP=2×FSDP=2 tests.
maxtext/src/maxtext/layers/moe.py, te_permutation.py, quantizations.py — reference for Maxtext's permutation, GMM dispatch, and quantization patterns.

content.text:
Excellent — I have a complete picture now. Let me write the grouped_dense POC, then provide the analysis.

 Now I have the full picture from the explore agent's findings. I need to update my todos and build out the grouped_dense proof of concept. The critical thing I'm seeing is how MoEBlock currently works: it has at most one shard_map (only present when expert parallelism is enabled), and within that shard_map, the entire forward pass executes as a pipeline—routing, dispatching, then three grouped_dense operations, combining results, and finally the tensor parallel scatter. There's no outer custom_vjp wrapping this.

The grouped_dense operation itself has a kernel_fsdp_info parameter, but here's the catch: its backward rule explicitly asserts that FSDP isn't supported, which is going to matter for what I'm testing. For the POC, I should focus on the FSDP plus expert parallelism scenario since that's what MoE actually uses. I'm thinking about the mesh layout with two axes—FSDP and expert parallelism—and how to shard the tokens, kernel weights, and group sizes across devices. But this is getting complicated fast, so let me strip it back and just focus on demonstrating the core grouped_dense behavior.

Actually, the real question is whether grouped_dense can even run inside a shard_map at all. Looking at the explore report, grouped_dense has its own custom_vjp and internally calls custom-call primitives like grouped_quantize and grouped_gemm. Inside a shard_map, those primitives run their implementations on local shards. The tricky part is that grouped_dense's kernel_fsdp_info is hardcoded to assert False, but in my POC where I'm controlling sharding through shard_map itself, I'd want kernel_fsdp_info to stay at its default so the FSDP gather happens in the shard_map layer instead.

For the actual POC, I'll use a single FSDP mesh with 8 devices, keep the expert dimension replicated for simplicity, and shard the K and N dimensions across FSDP. The tokens get sharded along the M dimension, and I need to figure out the right kernel sharding strategy—either sharding experts across FSDP or keeping them replicated depending on what makes sense for the test. I'm settling on a concrete design: mesh with 8 devices, kernel sharded across the expert dimension via FSDP so each device holds a subset of experts, and tokens batch-sharded so each device processes its own token slice. The key insight is that group_sizes also needs to be batch-sharded to match the token distribution, so each device only knows the routing for its local tokens and we all-gather the full kernel weights to compute the grouped dense operation. I'm settling on a simplified approach for the proof-of-concept: uniformly distribute tokens across experts so each device has the same local group sizes, which means I can just replicate the group_sizes array rather than computing it per-shard. With concrete numbers—64 total tokens, 8 experts, 8 FSDP shards—each device gets 8 tokens with 1 token per expert, making the math straightforward and letting me focus on the sharding mechanics without building a full router.

Now I'm sketching out the forward and backward pass implementations using shard_map, where the kernel gets all-gathered along the expert dimension...

Writing the shard_map functions...

Actually, I realize JAX can automatically generate the backward pass for shard_map by swapping the in/out specs, so I don't need to manually write the backward logic—I can just define the forward shard_map and let autodiff handle the rest through grouped_dense's custom_vjp. I'm realizing the auto-bwd shard_map will automatically handle the sharding transformations—JAX inserts reduce-scatter to put the kernel gradients back into FSDP layout after the all-gather in the forward pass, so the whole thing composes cleanly without manual bwd code.

For the POC structure, I should validate this by running grouped_dense fully replicated as a baseline, then inside a shard_map with FSDP sharding, and comparing both numerics and the HLO collectives. Since grouped_dense requires the full kernel shape and can't handle FSDP sharding directly (there's an assert blocking it), the shard_map pattern with all-gather is the right approach.

I'll follow the same design as the MoE block—using a single shard_map without wrapping the outer block in custom_vjp, letting each primitive handle its own autodiff. Now let me start writing the POC. the same layout as fwd inputs, so mirror specs are actually what you want for MoE backward passes. The custom_vjp + different-specs pattern we explored earlier is really only useful for unusual cases like mixing tensor parallelism in the forward with data parallelism in the backward, which doesn't apply here. So I'll stick with the single shardmap approach that matches the current MoE block design, write up the POC with numerical validation and HLO inspection, and explain why the two-shardmap pattern isn't necessary for this use case.

Now I'm thinking through the granularity question more carefully. You could theoretically split the forward into multiple shardmaps with different contracts at each phase—gate, router, dispatch, grouped_dense, combine—but that would introduce resharding overhead at the boundaries. The real question is whether we should split even within grouped_dense itself, separating the quantize and matmul operations. The answer is no, because they operate on the same tensor with consistent sharding, and splitting them would just force unnecessary reshapes. Better to keep them together in one shardmap or treat grouped_dense as a single custom primitive. But the comments mention reasons:- Cross-primitive collectives (all_gather inputs/logits → route → roll → FFN → psum_scatter)- axis_index for per-shard expert roll- Data-dependent token counts between primitives

These are reasons it MUST be one shardmap (custom_partitioning can't express them). But are there reasons within ONE direction (fwd) we might want multiple shardmaps?

I think the answer is YES if you want different sharding contracts at different phases, e.g.:- Gate: replicated
- Routing: data-parallel
- Dispatch: EP
- FFN: EP+FSDPBut each transition between contracts incurs reshardings.

Currently MoE has all in one shardmap because:- The intermediate state (gathered inputs, gathered logits, sparse_probs, routing_map, perm_state, group_sizes) flows naturally without re-sharding.
- The collectives are explicit at the boundaries (all_gather at start, psum_scatter at end).

If we split into multiple shardmaps, we'd have to choose where to break and explicitly handle the data flow. The question is whether the granularity unlock is worth the boundary cost.

I think the right framing is:- TRY multiple shardmaps where you genuinely want different sharding contracts.- Each split should be motivated by "this phase prefers a different sharding than the previous phase."- For the MoE block, the current ONE-shardmap design is adequate because the EP all-gather is the "phase boundary" and nothing else needs resharding.

Now I'm ready to move forward—I'll write the grouped_dense proof of concept first, then compare it against the current approach to analyze the tradeoffs around shardmap granularity.

input.contents:
"""POC #4: TE grouped_dense inside an FSDP shard_map (mimicking the MoE FFN).

This is the smallest possible smoke test for the *expert MLP* phase of the MoE
block. The router / dispatch / combine machinery is replaced by a synthetic
uniform routing (every expert gets the same number of tokens), so we can focus
purely on the question:

    Does TE's ``grouped_dense`` (which has its own ``custom_vjp`` wrapping
    a CUDA grouped-GEMM custom_call) compose correctly with our outer
    ``shard_map`` orchestration when the EXPERT-WEIGHT axis is FSDP-sharded?

Two variants are tested in one script:

  Variant A  -- ONE shard_map, NO outer custom_vjp.
                This is exactly the pattern the current MoEBlock follows
                (``_forward_ring_ep`` wraps a single ``shard_map`` and lets
                ``grouped_dense``'s own custom_vjp + JAX's autodiff produce
                the backward shard_map automatically with mirror in/out specs).

  Variant B  -- ONE outer custom_vjp + TWO shard_maps (one for fwd, one for
                bwd, written by us).
                This is the pattern from POC #1 / #2, allowing the bwd to use
                a *different* sharding contract from the fwd if we ever want
                to. For the FFN we don't need different specs, so this variant
                is mostly here to show the option exists and that it composes.

Both variants must produce identical numerical results to a single-device,
fully-replicated reference that calls ``grouped_dense`` directly with no
shard_map at all.

Layout (FSDP across ``fsdp`` axis, no EP for this POC):

    Mesh: ('fsdp',) with N devices.
    x         : P('fsdp', None)       on (M, K)        # batch-sharded tokens
    kernel    : P('fsdp', None, None) on (E, K, N)     # experts FSDP-sharded
    grp_sizes : P('fsdp',)            on (E,)          # per-shard local counts
    y         : P('fsdp', None)       on (M, N)        # batch-sharded output

Inside the fwd shard_map, each device:
    1. ``all_gather`` the kernel along 'fsdp' (axis 0 = E) -> full (E, K, N).
    2. Call ``grouped_dense(x_local, kernel_full, group_sizes_local)``.
    3. Output is each device's own (M/fsdp, N) slice -- no combine needed
       because tokens never crossed devices in this synthetic setup.

The autodiff'd backward (Variant A) or hand-written backward (Variant B):
    1. Same ``all_gather`` of kernel.
    2. ``grouped_dense.bwd`` produces dx (batch-sharded) and dW (full (E, K, N)
       partial across 'fsdp' shards).
    3. ``psum_scatter`` of dW along 'fsdp' (axis 0) -> back to FSDP layout.

Run:
    python jax_poc_grouped_dense_fsdp.py
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import os
import sys
import re

os.environ.setdefault("XLA_FLAGS", "--xla_force_host_platform_device_count=4")

_SCRIPT_DIR = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
sys.path[:] = [p for p in sys.path if os.path.abspath(p or os.getcwd()) != _SCRIPT_DIR]

from functools import partial

import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp

if hasattr(jax, "shard_map"):
    _shard_map_impl = jax.shard_map
else:
    from jax.experimental.shard_map import shard_map as _shard_map_impl  # type: ignore[no-redef]


def shard_map(f, *, mesh, in_specs, out_specs):
    return _shard_map_impl(f, mesh=mesh, in_specs=in_specs, out_specs=out_specs)


from jax.sharding import Mesh, PartitionSpec as P, NamedSharding

import numpy as np

# --- TE imports ---------------------------------------------------------
try:
    from transformer_engine.jax.dense import grouped_dense
    from transformer_engine.jax.quantize import noop_quantizer_set
    HAVE_TE = True
except Exception as e:  # noqa: BLE001
    print(f"FATAL: transformer_engine.jax not importable -- {e}")
    raise


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Mesh
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEVICES = jax.devices()
FSDP_SIZE = len(DEVICES)
MESH = Mesh(np.asarray(DEVICES).reshape(FSDP_SIZE), axis_names=("fsdp",))
FSDP_AXIS = "fsdp"


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Synthetic uniform routing helper
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# In a real MoE block, the dispatch step produces:
#   - a permuted activation tensor (M, K) sorted by expert
#   - a group_sizes vector (E,) where group_sizes[i] = #tokens going to expert i
# For this POC we skip the dispatch and synthetically arrange tokens so each
# expert gets the same number of tokens, on each FSDP shard. That means:
#   - group_sizes_local = [tokens_per_expert_per_shard] * E  (same on each shard)
#   - x is batch-sharded along 'fsdp', sized so M/fsdp/E is an integer.

def _make_uniform_grouped_inputs(rng, M, K, N, E, dtype=jnp.float32):
    """Make (x, kernel, group_sizes) for uniform per-expert routing."""
    rx, rk = jax.random.split(rng)
    x = jax.random.normal(rx, (M, K), dtype=dtype)
    # Init with 1/sqrt(K) so outputs stay O(1) and we can use tight tolerances.
    kernel = jax.random.normal(rk, (E, K, N), dtype=dtype) * (1.0 / np.sqrt(K))
    tokens_per_expert = M // E
    group_sizes = jnp.full((E,), tokens_per_expert, dtype=jnp.int32)
    return x, kernel, group_sizes


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Reference: single-device grouped_dense (no shard_map, fully replicated)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def grouped_dense_reference(x, kernel, group_sizes):
    # contracting_dims=((1,),(1,)) means: x.dim 1 (K) contracts with kernel.dim 1 (K).
    # Default for grouped_dense; explicit here for clarity.
    return grouped_dense(
        x,
        kernel,
        group_sizes,
        contracting_dims=((1,), (1,)),
        quantizer_set=noop_quantizer_set,
        kernel_fsdp_info=(None, -1),  # we handle FSDP outside; tell GD not to.
    )


# ===========================================================================
# Variant A: ONE shard_map, no outer custom_vjp.
#   This is exactly the pattern the current MoEBlock follows
#   (one shard_map wraps the body, JAX autodiff produces the bwd shard_map
#    with mirror specs, and grouped_dense's own custom_vjp handles the
#    primitive-level gradient).
# ===========================================================================

def _ffn_shard_fn_A(x, kernel, group_sizes_local):
    # FSDP all-gather of expert weights along the E (axis=0) dim.
    kernel_full = jax.lax.all_gather(
        kernel, axis_name=FSDP_AXIS, axis=0, tiled=True
    )
    # Each shard now sees all experts and only its own tokens.
    y = grouped_dense(
        x,
        kernel_full,
        group_sizes_local,
        contracting_dims=((1,), (1,)),
        quantizer_set=noop_quantizer_set,
        kernel_fsdp_info=(None, -1),
    )
    return y


def ffn_block_A(x, kernel, group_sizes_local):
    sm = shard_map(
        _ffn_shard_fn_A,
        mesh=MESH,
        in_specs=(
            P(FSDP_AXIS, None),         # x batch-sharded
            P(FSDP_AXIS, None, None),   # kernel sharded along expert dim
            P(FSDP_AXIS,),              # group_sizes -- per-shard local counts
        ),
        out_specs=P(FSDP_AXIS, None),   # y batch-sharded
    )
    return sm(x, kernel, group_sizes_local)


# ===========================================================================
# Variant B: ONE outer custom_vjp + TWO shard_maps (fwd + bwd written by us).
#   Demonstrates the option to give the bwd a *different* sharding contract.
#   For this FFN we keep the bwd contract identical to the fwd (mirror specs),
#   so the only thing different from Variant A is who controls the bwd
#   shard_map -- us (Variant B) vs. JAX autodiff (Variant A).
# ===========================================================================

def _ffn_fwd_shard_fn_B(x, kernel, group_sizes_local):
    """Same kernel as Variant A's fwd. Save residuals for our bwd."""
    kernel_full = jax.lax.all_gather(
        kernel, axis_name=FSDP_AXIS, axis=0, tiled=True
    )
    y = grouped_dense(
        x,
        kernel_full,
        group_sizes_local,
        contracting_dims=((1,), (1,)),
        quantizer_set=noop_quantizer_set,
        kernel_fsdp_info=(None, -1),
    )
    return y


def _ffn_bwd_shard_fn_B(x, kernel, group_sizes_local, dy):
    """Backward kernel.

    We don't manually re-implement grouped_dense's bwd -- we just call
    ``jax.vjp(grouped_dense, ...)`` to get its bwd as a JAX function and
    then pipe ``dy`` through it. That lets us reuse all of TE's grouped-GEMM
    bwd logic while still controlling the shard_map's in/out specs.
    """
    kernel_full = jax.lax.all_gather(
        kernel, axis_name=FSDP_AXIS, axis=0, tiled=True
    )

    # vjp gives us the backward closure for grouped_dense.
    def _gd(x_arg, kernel_arg):
        return grouped_dense(
            x_arg,
            kernel_arg,
            group_sizes_local,
            contracting_dims=((1,), (1,)),
            quantizer_set=noop_quantizer_set,
            kernel_fsdp_info=(None, -1),
        )
    _, gd_vjp = jax.vjp(_gd, x, kernel_full)
    dx, dkernel_full = gd_vjp(dy)
    # dx        : (M/fsdp, K)               batch-sharded -- matches x's spec
    # dkernel_full: (E, K, N) PARTIAL across 'fsdp' (each shard's contribution
    #               from its own tokens). We psum_scatter to land it back in
    #               the FSDP layout (E/fsdp, K, N) per shard.
    dkernel = jax.lax.psum_scatter(
        dkernel_full, axis_name=FSDP_AXIS, scatter_dimension=0, tiled=True
    )

    return dx, dkernel, None  # group_sizes is not differentiable


@jax.custom_vjp
def ffn_block_B(x, kernel, group_sizes_local):
    y, _ = _ffn_block_B_fwd_rule(x, kernel, group_sizes_local)
    return y


def _ffn_block_B_fwd_rule(x, kernel, group_sizes_local):
    fwd_sm = shard_map(
        _ffn_fwd_shard_fn_B,
        mesh=MESH,
        in_specs=(
            P(FSDP_AXIS, None),
            P(FSDP_AXIS, None, None),
            P(FSDP_AXIS,),
        ),
        out_specs=P(FSDP_AXIS, None),
    )
    y = fwd_sm(x, kernel, group_sizes_local)
    return y, (x, kernel, group_sizes_local)


def _ffn_block_B_bwd_rule(residuals, dy):
    x, kernel, group_sizes_local = residuals
    bwd_sm = shard_map(
        _ffn_bwd_shard_fn_B,
        mesh=MESH,
        in_specs=(
            P(FSDP_AXIS, None),
            P(FSDP_AXIS, None, None),
            P(FSDP_AXIS,),
            P(FSDP_AXIS, None),         # dy batch-sharded (mirrors y)
        ),
        out_specs=(
            P(FSDP_AXIS, None),         # dx -- mirrors x
            P(FSDP_AXIS, None, None),   # dkernel -- mirrors kernel
            P(FSDP_AXIS,),              # group_sizes grad placeholder
        ),
    )
    dx, dkernel, dgs = bwd_sm(x, kernel, group_sizes_local, dy)
    return dx, dkernel, dgs


ffn_block_B.defvjp(_ffn_block_B_fwd_rule, _ffn_block_B_bwd_rule)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helpers (HLO collective counter, parity report)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

_COLLECTIVE_KINDS = (
    "all-reduce", "all-reduce-start",
    "all-gather", "all-gather-start",
    "all-to-all", "all-to-all-start",
    "reduce-scatter", "reduce-scatter-start",
)


def _summarize_collectives(hlo_text):
    counts = {k: 0 for k in _COLLECTIVE_KINDS}
    for kind in _COLLECTIVE_KINDS:
        pat = re.compile(r"\s" + re.escape(kind) + r"\(")
        counts[kind] = len(pat.findall(hlo_text))
    logical = {
        "all-reduce":     counts["all-reduce"]     + counts["all-reduce-start"],
        "all-gather":     counts["all-gather"]     + counts["all-gather-start"],
        "all-to-all":     counts["all-to-all"]     + counts["all-to-all-start"],
        "reduce-scatter": counts["reduce-scatter"] + counts["reduce-scatter-start"],
    }
    return logical


def _save_hlo(hlo_text, name):
    out = os.path.join(_SCRIPT_DIR, f"{name}.hlo.txt")
    with open(out, "w") as f:
        f.write(hlo_text)
    return out


def _allclose_report(name, a, b, rtol=1e-3, atol=1e-3):
    a = np.asarray(a, dtype=np.float64)
    b = np.asarray(b, dtype=np.float64)
    abs_diff = np.max(np.abs(a - b))
    denom = np.maximum(np.max(np.abs(a)), np.max(np.abs(b)))
    rel_diff = abs_diff / max(denom, 1e-12)
    ok = abs_diff <= atol + rtol * denom
    return ok, f"{name}: max_abs={abs_diff:.3e}  max_rel={rel_diff:.3e}  (ok={ok})"


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Driver
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def _loss_fn(fn, x, kernel, group_sizes_local):
    y = fn(x, kernel, group_sizes_local)
    return jnp.sum(y * y)


def main():
    jax.config.update("jax_default_matmul_precision", "highest")

    print(f"JAX devices ({len(DEVICES)}): {[str(d) for d in DEVICES]}")
    print(f"Mesh: {MESH}\n")

    # Sizes: every expert gets the same #tokens per shard.
    # Constraints:
    #   * E divisible by FSDP_SIZE (we shard experts across fsdp)
    #   * M divisible by E*FSDP_SIZE  (tokens_per_expert_per_shard is integer)
    E = 8 * FSDP_SIZE if FSDP_SIZE <= 4 else 8
    while E % FSDP_SIZE != 0:
        E += 1
    tokens_per_expert_per_shard = 4
    M = E * FSDP_SIZE * tokens_per_expert_per_shard
    K, N = 32, 64

    print(f"Shapes: M={M}, K={K}, N={N}, E={E}, FSDP={FSDP_SIZE}")
    print(f"  per-shard:")
    print(f"    x      : ({M // FSDP_SIZE}, {K})")
    print(f"    kernel : ({E // FSDP_SIZE}, {K}, {N})  (E/fsdp experts per shard)")
    print(f"    y      : ({M // FSDP_SIZE}, {N})")
    print(f"  group_sizes_local: [{tokens_per_expert_per_shard}] * {E}\n")

    rng = jax.random.PRNGKey(0)
    x_full, kernel_full, group_sizes_global = _make_uniform_grouped_inputs(
        rng, M, K, N, E
    )

    # Place inputs:
    x_sh    = NamedSharding(MESH, P(FSDP_AXIS, None))
    k_sh    = NamedSharding(MESH, P(FSDP_AXIS, None, None))
    rep     = NamedSharding(MESH, P())
    gs_sh   = NamedSharding(MESH, P(FSDP_AXIS,))

    x  = jax.device_put(x_full, x_sh)
    kernel = jax.device_put(kernel_full, k_sh)

    # group_sizes_local: per-shard, same on each shard. Construct by tiling
    # the per-shard vector across 'fsdp'. Each shard sees [tpe_per_shard]*E.
    group_sizes_local_per_shard = jnp.full(
        (E,), tokens_per_expert_per_shard, dtype=jnp.int32
    )
    group_sizes_tiled = jnp.tile(group_sizes_local_per_shard, FSDP_SIZE)
    group_sizes_local = jax.device_put(group_sizes_tiled, gs_sh)

    # For the reference we need the global group_sizes describing the FULL x.
    group_sizes_global = jnp.full((E,), M // E, dtype=jnp.int32)
    x_rep      = jax.device_put(x_full,            rep)
    kernel_rep = jax.device_put(kernel_full,       rep)
    gs_rep     = jax.device_put(group_sizes_global, rep)

    print("--- per-device shapes the USER SUPPLIES ---")
    print(f"  x       : global={x.shape}      "
          f"per-device={x.addressable_shards[0].data.shape}      "
          f"spec={x.sharding.spec}")
    print(f"  kernel  : global={kernel.shape} "
          f"per-device={kernel.addressable_shards[0].data.shape} "
          f"spec={kernel.sharding.spec}")
    print(f"  grp_loc : global={group_sizes_local.shape}     "
          f"per-device={group_sizes_local.addressable_shards[0].data.shape}      "
          f"spec={group_sizes_local.sharding.spec}")

    # ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    # Reference: single-device, fully replicated grouped_dense.
    # ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    fwd_ref = jax.jit(grouped_dense_reference)
    y_ref = fwd_ref(x_rep, kernel_rep, gs_rep)
    grad_ref = jax.jit(jax.grad(
        partial(_loss_fn, grouped_dense_reference), argnums=(0, 1)
    ))
    dx_r, dkernel_r = grad_ref(x_rep, kernel_rep, gs_rep)

    # ======================================================================
    # Variant A: shard_map only, no outer custom_vjp
    # ======================================================================
    print("\n" + "=" * 72)
    print("Variant A: ONE shard_map, no outer custom_vjp (mirrors current MoEBlock)")
    print("=" * 72)

    fwd_A = jax.jit(ffn_block_A)
    y_A = fwd_A(x, kernel, group_sizes_local)
    print(f"  y_A : global={y_A.shape}  "
          f"per-device={y_A.addressable_shards[0].data.shape}  "
          f"spec={y_A.sharding.spec}")

    grad_A = jax.jit(jax.grad(
        partial(_loss_fn, ffn_block_A), argnums=(0, 1)
    ))
    dx_A, dkernel_A = grad_A(x, kernel, group_sizes_local)
    print(f"  dx_A      : global={dx_A.shape}      "
          f"per-device={dx_A.addressable_shards[0].data.shape}      "
          f"spec={dx_A.sharding.spec}")
    print(f"  dkernel_A : global={dkernel_A.shape} "
          f"per-device={dkernel_A.addressable_shards[0].data.shape} "
          f"spec={dkernel_A.sharding.spec}")

    print("\n  --- numerical parity vs. replicated reference ---")
    for ok, msg in [
        _allclose_report("y_A      ", y_A,       y_ref),
        _allclose_report("dx_A     ", dx_A,      dx_r),
        _allclose_report("dkernel_A", dkernel_A, dkernel_r),
    ]:
        print(f"  {msg}")
        if not ok:
            raise AssertionError(f"Variant A parity failed for {msg}")

    hlo_A = jax.jit(jax.value_and_grad(
        partial(_loss_fn, ffn_block_A), argnums=(0, 1)
    )).lower(x, kernel, group_sizes_local).compile().as_text()
    pa = _save_hlo(hlo_A, "grouped_dense_variant_A")
    cA = _summarize_collectives(hlo_A)
    print(f"\n  HLO -> {pa}")
    print(f"  Logical collectives: all-reduce={cA['all-reduce']} "
          f"all-gather={cA['all-gather']} "
          f"reduce-scatter={cA['reduce-scatter']} "
          f"all-to-all={cA['all-to-all']}")

    # ======================================================================
    # Variant B: outer custom_vjp + two shard_maps (fwd + bwd)
    # ======================================================================
    print("\n" + "=" * 72)
    print("Variant B: outer custom_vjp + two shard_maps (POC #1/#2 pattern)")
    print("=" * 72)

    fwd_B = jax.jit(ffn_block_B)
    y_B = fwd_B(x, kernel, group_sizes_local)
    print(f"  y_B : global={y_B.shape}  "
          f"per-device={y_B.addressable_shards[0].data.shape}  "
          f"spec={y_B.sharding.spec}")

    grad_B = jax.jit(jax.grad(
        partial(_loss_fn, ffn_block_B), argnums=(0, 1)
    ))
    dx_B, dkernel_B = grad_B(x, kernel, group_sizes_local)
    print(f"  dx_B      : global={dx_B.shape}      "
          f"per-device={dx_B.addressable_shards[0].data.shape}      "
          f"spec={dx_B.sharding.spec}")
    print(f"  dkernel_B : global={dkernel_B.shape} "
          f"per-device={dkernel_B.addressable_shards[0].data.shape} "
          f"spec={dkernel_B.sharding.spec}")

    print("\n  --- numerical parity vs. replicated reference ---")
    for ok, msg in [
        _allclose_report("y_B      ", y_B,       y_ref),
        _allclose_report("dx_B     ", dx_B,      dx_r),
        _allclose_report("dkernel_B", dkernel_B, dkernel_r),
    ]:
        print(f"  {msg}")
        if not ok:
            raise AssertionError(f"Variant B parity failed for {msg}")

    hlo_B = jax.jit(jax.value_and_grad(
        partial(_loss_fn, ffn_block_B), argnums=(0, 1)
    )).lower(x, kernel, group_sizes_local).compile().as_text()
    pb = _save_hlo(hlo_B, "grouped_dense_variant_B")
    cB = _summarize_collectives(hlo_B)
    print(f"\n  HLO -> {pb}")
    print(f"  Logical collectives: all-reduce={cB['all-reduce']} "
          f"all-gather={cB['all-gather']} "
          f"reduce-scatter={cB['reduce-scatter']} "
          f"all-to-all={cB['all-to-all']}")

    # ======================================================================
    # A vs B comparison
    # ======================================================================
    print("\n" + "=" * 72)
    print("Variant A vs. Variant B comparison")
    print("=" * 72)
    print(f"  Same y     : {bool(jnp.allclose(y_A, y_B, atol=1e-5, rtol=1e-5))}")
    print(f"  Same dx    : {bool(jnp.allclose(dx_A, dx_B, atol=1e-5, rtol=1e-5))}")
    print(f"  Same dW    : {bool(jnp.allclose(dkernel_A, dkernel_B, atol=1e-5, rtol=1e-5))}")
    print(f"  HLO collectives:")
    print(f"    A: all-reduce={cA['all-reduce']} all-gather={cA['all-gather']} "
          f"reduce-scatter={cA['reduce-scatter']} all-to-all={cA['all-to-all']}")
    print(f"    B: all-reduce={cB['all-reduce']} all-gather={cB['all-gather']} "
          f"reduce-scatter={cB['reduce-scatter']} all-to-all={cB['all-to-all']}")
    print()
    print("  Interpretation:")
    print("    * If the collective counts match, the explicit-bwd-shard_map (B)")
    print("      gives no perf advantage over the autodiff'd-bwd-shard_map (A)")
    print("      for THIS workload (because we picked mirror specs).")
    print("    * Variant B's value would only show up if the bwd benefited from")
    print("      a *different* sharding contract -- e.g. recompute differently,")
    print("      reorder collectives, or shard along a different mesh axis.")

    print("\nGrouped-dense FSDP POC PASSED (both variants).")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

todos.content: Write grouped_dense POC following the same shard_map+custom_vjp pattern
Compare MoEBlock to POC pattern and answer the granularity question

content.text:
POC #4 (`jax_poc_grouped_dense_fsdp.py`) is ready. Now let me give you the synthesis: how the current MoEBlock compares to our POC pattern, and whether more granularity than "1 shardmap per direction" would help.

## How the current MoEBlock compares to our POC pattern

| Aspect | POC #1 / #2 | Current MoEBlock |
|---|---|---|
| **Outer `custom_vjp`** | Yes — `mlp_block` wraps the whole block | **No** — `MoEBlock.__call__` just runs Flax operations; gradients flow through each underlying primitive's own `custom_vjp` (`grouped_dense`, `_sort_activations`, `token_dispatch/combine`, `_fused_topk_with_score_function`) |
| **Number of shard_maps per direction** | 1 fwd + 1 bwd (both written by us) | 0 fwd + 0 bwd if EP is off; **1 fwd inside `_forward_ring_ep`** if EP is on. The bwd shard_map is auto-generated by JAX with mirror specs |
| **Where collectives live** | Inside our shard_maps (explicit `psum`, `all_gather`, `psum_scatter`) | Inside the one EP shard_map (explicit) **+ inside each TE primitive** (custom_partitioning auto-inserts collectives based on input sharding) |
| **What governs sharding** | `in_specs` / `out_specs` of each shard_map | A mix of: (a) per-primitive `custom_partitioning` rules, (b) `with_logical_partitioning` + `with_sharding_constraint_by_logical_axes` annotations, (c) the one EP shard_map's `in_specs/out_specs` |

**Key observation:** the MoEBlock corresponds to **Variant A** of POC #4 (one shard_map, no outer custom_vjp), not to the two-shardmap pattern from POC #1 / #2. This is the right choice for MoE because:

1. The bwd doesn't want a different sharding contract from the fwd — mirror specs are exactly what you want (tokens come back through the same dispatch boundary, weight grads land in the same FSDP layout).
2. Wrapping the whole block in a single `custom_vjp` would force you to **re-implement every primitive's bwd** by hand, losing TE's grouped-GEMM bwd, the topk vjp, the sort bwd, etc.
3. JAX's autodiff through `shard_map` produces a backward shard_map with mirror in/out specs **for free** — that's a major reason `shard_map` exists.

The two-shardmap pattern from POCs #1 / #2 is genuinely useful when you want **fundamentally different parallelism in fwd vs bwd** (e.g. POC #1's TP fwd / DP bwd). For MoE, you don't, so the simpler pattern is correct.

## Granularity: should we have multiple shardmaps per direction?

The question is whether to split the single MoE forward shardmap into multiple shardmaps with different sharding contracts at different phases. Let me lay out the four phases and what each one *would* want if we split:

| Phase | Inputs | Outputs | Natural sharding |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gate** (`x → logits`) | `x: (B, S, H)` | `logits: (B, S, E)` | Whatever `x` is (already sharded). Computed on each shard locally. |
| **Router** (`logits → routing_map, sparse_probs`) | `logits` | `routing_map: (B*S, E)`, `sparse_probs: (B*S, E)` | Replicated across the shard's view (data-local op) |
| **Dispatch** (`x → permuted_x, group_sizes`) | `x, routing_map` | `permuted_x: (M, H)`, `group_sizes: (E,)` | EP boundary: tokens reorganized so each expert's tokens are contiguous |
| **Expert FFN** (3 × `grouped_dense`) | `permuted_x, wi_0, wi_1, wo` | `expert_out: (M, H)` | FSDP on weights (gather just-in-time), tokens still EP-local |
| **Combine** | `expert_out, perm_state` | `combined: (B, S, H)` | Tokens scattered back to original positions |
| **TP scatter (final)** | `combined` | `(B, S, H/tp)` | TP-sharded if TP is on |

Currently **all 6 phases live in one big shard_map** inside `_forward_ring_ep`. Splitting them into multiple shard_maps **would buy granularity** in two specific ways:

### Where granularity could help

1. **Phase-specific sharding axes.** Say you want the gate to be TP-sharded, the router to be replicated, the dispatch to be EP-sharded, and the expert FFN to be FSDP+EP+TP. Today that's all expressed implicitly via the *one* shard_map's `in_specs` + per-primitive partitioning. With multiple shard_maps you'd express it as a contract per phase. **Benefit**: easier to read, easier to override one phase's sharding without affecting others. **Cost**: phase-boundary reshardings (XLA-inserted) — but you'd want those boundaries anyway if the contracts differ.

2. **Different fwd vs bwd contracts at a specific phase.** The two-shardmap-per-phase pattern from POC #1 lets you shard differently in fwd vs bwd. The most plausible use in MoE is the **dispatch phase**: fwd dispatches tokens via `all_to_all`, bwd combines them via `all_to_all`. Today these are different *primitives* (`token_dispatch` and `token_combine`) each with their own `custom_vjp`, so they already implement this asymmetry — no extra shard_maps needed. So this lever doesn't add much for MoE.

### Where granularity is wasted (or harmful)

3. **Within one phase: quantize + GEMM.** You asked specifically about `grouped_dense` (which is internally `grouped_quantize` then `grouped_gemm`). Splitting these into two shard_maps would force a reshape/reshard at the boundary, and there's nothing different about the sharding contract you'd want for the quantize vs. the GEMM step. **The right level of granularity is the `grouped_dense` primitive itself**, which already wraps quantize + GEMM in one `custom_vjp` and one `custom_partitioning`. Don't split below this.

4. **Within one phase: inside the FFN's three GEMMs.** The three `grouped_dense` calls (wi_0, wi_1, wo) all want the same sharding. A single shard_map covering all three is right; splitting each into its own shard_map would just add boundary reshardings.

### My recommendation

For the MoE block as currently designed, **the granularity is right**: one shard_map for the EP body, with the FSDP all-gather of weights and the per-primitive `custom_partitioning` doing the work *within* the body. Splitting further would add complexity without enabling a sharding pattern the current design can't express.

The **one place granularity might help** is splitting along the EP dispatch boundary:

```
shard_map_1 (input prep + gate + router)   in_specs: per-EP-shard tokens
                                            out_specs: per-EP-shard tokens + routing
        ↓ implicit reshard (or no-op if specs align)
shard_map_2 (dispatch + expert FFN + combine)   in_specs: dispatched layout
                                                 out_specs: undispatched
        ↓
shard_map_3 (TP scatter / output)
```

This would let you, e.g., experiment with running the gate on a different mesh axis from the FFN. **But** the current single-shardmap design lets you do the same thing by choosing your `in_specs` / `out_specs` carefully — it's just less *legible*.

## Mapping your two planned experiments to this analysis

**Experiment 1 — Quantize before FSDP all-gather** (FP8 weights gathered instead of BF16):

This is **not** a granularity-of-shard_maps question — it's a question about how `grouped_dense` does its FSDP gather. Currently `grouped_dense` asserts `kernel_fsdp_info` is empty (it doesn't handle FSDP itself; XLA does it implicitly before quantize). The fix is *inside* `grouped_dense` (or in TE's gemm primitive's `partition` callback), not at the shard_map level. **POC #4's Variant A is the right model** — your block-level shard_map calls `grouped_dense`, and `grouped_dense` internally decides whether to quantize-then-gather (FP8) or gather-then-quantize (BF16). The MoE block doesn't need to change for this experiment; only `grouped_dense` and its FFI does.

**Experiment 2 — Weight layout & FSDP shard axis** (eliminating `loop_transpose_fusion`):

Same story — this is a knob on `grouped_dense` (which axis is contracting, which is FSDP-sharded), exposed via `kernel_fsdp_info` and the kernel shape itself. The shard_map structure is unaffected; you'd just pass differently-shaped/sharded `wi_0/wi_1/wo` tensors through. This is also a **config sweep**, not a structural change to the MoEBlock.

So neither planned experiment requires more shard_maps. Both want changes *inside* `grouped_dense` (and possibly the `grouped_gemm` C++ primitive's partitioning rule). The MoEBlock's current shard_map structure is the right scaffold for both.

## Recommended next steps

todos.content: Compare MoEBlock to POC pattern and answer the granularity question

content.text:
When you get a system, I'd suggest running things in this order:

1. **Run `jax_poc_grouped_dense_fsdp.py`** to confirm both Variants A and B work numerically and see the HLO collective counts. The expected outcome: identical results for A and B, identical (or near-identical) collective counts. That validates the smallest-possible MoE FFN scaffold.

2. **Skip a "MoE block POC"** — the existing MoEBlock in `transformer_engine/jax/flax/moe.py` already *is* that POC. Run the existing `tests/jax/test_distributed_moe_block.py` (EP=2 × FSDP=2) — that's the right end-to-end validation.

3. **Tackle Experiment 1 inside `grouped_dense`**, not the MoEBlock. The change is to thread `kernel_fsdp_info` through `_grouped_dense_fwd_rule` and `_grouped_dense_bwd_rule`, replacing the `assert not kernel_fsdp_enabled` with logic that does:
   - Local FP8 quantize on the per-shard kernel slice.
   - All-gather the resulting `ScaledTensor` (data + scales) along the FSDP axis.
   - Use the gathered FP8 tensor in `grouped_gemm`.
   The MoEBlock just adds a `quantize_before_fsdp_ag: bool` knob and forwards `kernel_fsdp_info=(self.fsdp_axis, expert_axis)` to the three `grouped_dense` calls. That's a small, contained change.

4. **Defer Experiment 2** until you've discussed the API design (per the handoff notes). The current `wi_kernel_axes` / `wo_kernel_axes` already lets you sweep layouts; the question is mostly about whether `grouped_dense`'s `custom_partitioning` rule cooperates with all permutations and whether the `loop_transpose_fusion` actually disappears for the optimal layout — empirical, profile-driven.

The takeaway from this session: **the MoEBlock's shard_map structure is correct and the right level of granularity for the experiments you've planned**. The remaining work is in `grouped_dense` (Experiment 1) and the layout sweep (Experiment 2), not in restructuring the MoEBlock itself.

This is a TE/Shardy compatibility bug — `GroupedGemmPrimitive`'s `shardy_sharding_rule` callback returns a rule for 1 operand but the primitive actually has 13 operands (the FFI ABI of grouped GEMM takes many tensors). It fires even on the **reference** call (`grouped_dense` called with replicated tensors, no shard_map at all), so it's not about our sharding — it's TE's Shardy rule for grouped_gemm being incomplete.

The simplest fix: disable Shardy and fall back to the older GSPMD partitioner (which uses TE's `infer_sharding_from_operands` + `partition` callbacks, which TE has fully implemented). JAX has two SPMD partitioners, and TE's grouped GEMM only has the GSPMD one fully wired up.

Let me check the right flag name and add the workaround.

 I'm looking at a TE bug where the sharding rule for `grouped_gemm`'s custom partitioning expects 1 operand but the actual primitive has 13, which suggests a mismatch in how the `shardy_sharding_rule` is defined in GroupedGemmPrimitive.

The error originates from JAX's custom_partitioning code when it tries to convert the user-provided shardy rule to MLIR. Interestingly, the user is hitting this even on the reference implementation with replicated tensors outside any shard_map, so the issue is in the primitive's shardy rule itself rather than the shard_map setup.

The quickest workaround would be to disable Shardy and fall back to the older GSPMD partitioner using a JAX config flag, though I need to verify the exact flag name since JAX has been transitioning between these partitioners.

Looking more closely, the root cause is that `GroupedGemmPrimitive`'s shardy_sharding_rule returns a rule with only 1 operand while the primitive itself has 13 operands due to the FFI ABI for grouped GEMM. This is a bug in TE's shardy rule definition. The user can work around this by disabling Shardy via config or environment variable, or by reporting the issue to TE. Let me update the POC with the workaround and clarify the difference between GSPMD and Shardy partitioners.

Now I need to check the GroupedGemmPrimitive's shardy rule to see if it's actually broken with 13 operands, though there might be a simpler approach here.

The straightforward fix is probably just disabling the Shardy partitioner with a comment explaining why, then I should verify what version of grouped GEMM TE is using.

Confirmed: `GroupedGemmPrimitive` (line 1427+) has 13+ operands but **TE only defines `infer_sharding_from_operands` and `partition` (GSPMD)** for it, not a `shardy_sharding_rule`. JAX's auto-generated default Shardy rule assumes 1 operand and fails at lowering. The POC needs to disable Shardy and use GSPMD.

Let me fix the POC:

 So `GroupedGemmPrimitive` takes over 13 inputs with all those dimension parameters, bias, and scale factors, but TE only implements the GSPMD sharding rules—there's no `shardy_sharding_rule` defined for it. When Shardy gets enabled in JAX, this becomes a problem.

JAX tries to apply a default sharding rule that assumes a single operand maps to the output, but with 13 operands the mismatch causes the lowering to fail. The solution is to disable Shardy for now and update the POC accordingly.

Edit landed cleanly. Quick syntax sanity check:

Both files still parse. Re-run the POC and the `ValueError` should be gone.

### Why this happened (short version)

The error fires during MLIR lowering of TE's `te_grouped_gemm_ffi` custom-call:

- **TE's `GroupedGemmPrimitive`** (`gemm.py:1427`, your local checkout) takes 13+ FFI operands: `lhs_data, lhs_scale_inv, rhs_data, rhs_scale_inv, bias, lhs_first_dims, lhs_last_dims, rhs_first_dims, rhs_last_dims, out_first_dims, out_last_dims, alpha, beta, …`. (See the comment on line 1433–1435.)
- TE provides the **GSPMD** partitioner methods (`infer_sharding_from_operands`, `partition`) for it but **does not provide a `shardy_sharding_rule`** for grouped GEMM. (The single-tensor `GemmPrimitive.shardy_sharding_rule` at line 1145 exists, but `GroupedGemmPrimitive` has no analogue.)
- When the **Shardy** partitioner is active, JAX synthesises a default 1-operand sharding rule on the fly (one input → one output). At lowering time it checks rule arity vs. op arity, sees `1 != 13`, and bails with the exact message you saw — even before any of our shard_map / FSDP work runs (that's why it triggered on the *replicated reference* call too).

### The fix

Force JAX to use the older **GSPMD** partitioner, which TE *does* fully support for grouped_gemm:

```python
os.environ.setdefault("JAX_USE_SHARDY_PARTITIONER", "false")
# …
import jax
jax.config.update("jax_use_shardy_partitioner", False)
```

I added this to both `jax_poc_grouped_dense_fsdp.py` and `jax_poc_fsdp_te_dense.py` (the dense POC currently happens to be Shardy-clean, but adding the flag is harmless and will protect us if we touch other TE primitives later).

### Implications for the MoEBlock work

This is worth flagging upstream — **any user running `MoEBlock` (or anything that calls `grouped_dense`) with Shardy enabled will hit this**. Two ways to address it inside TE proper:

1. **Add a `shardy_sharding_rule` staticmethod on `GroupedGemmPrimitive`** that lists all 13+ operands explicitly (mirroring what the GSPMD `infer_sharding_from_operands` already encodes). This is the long-term fix.
2. Until (1) lands, document that TE JAX requires `JAX_USE_SHARDY_PARTITIONER=false` (or equivalently, that distributed TE tests need to opt out of Shardy — note `tests/jax/test_distributed_router.py:60` *opts in* to Shardy, which suggests TE has been exercising Shardy on simpler primitives only).

Once you've confirmed POC #4 runs end-to-end with this patch, I can:
- inspect the dumped HLO for both Variants A and B to verify the FSDP collective pattern around `grouped_gemm` matches what we saw in POC #2/#3, and
- start drafting the **Experiment 1 ("quantize before FSDP all-gather")** changes to `grouped_dense`'s `kernel_fsdp_info` plumbing in `transformer_engine/jax/dense.py` — that's the next concrete edit on the path to the actual MoEBlock change.

Different error now — GSPMD's path is firing but TE's `infer_sharding_from_operands` callback is `None`. So the issue is that `GroupedGemmPrimitive` is registered with `custom_partitioning` but **neither** the Shardy rule nor the GSPMD callbacks are populated. Let me dig:

 After disabling Shardy, we're hitting a different error with GSPMD where `_custom_partitioning_infer_sharding_from_operands` is being called but TE's `GroupedGemmPrimitive` has `infer_sharding_from_operands` set to `None`, meaning neither sharding framework is properly configured for this primitive.

Let me dig into how `GroupedGemmPrimitive` is actually set up with custom partitioning to see if they're using it in a special context rather than directly.

I'm seeing that `register_primitive` checks whether `infer_sharding_from_operands` is directly defined on the class rather than inherited, and falls back to a default abstract method if it's not. Since `GroupedGemmPrimitive` doesn't define any of these methods—`infer_sharding_from_operands`, `partition`, or `shardy_sharding_rule`—it relies entirely on those defaults.

So the primitive has no custom partitioning rules and should fall through, but the GSPMD path is somehow returning None instead. I need to dig into base.py to see what's actually happening there.

Let me check the JAX version — the comment at base.py:22 says GSPMD callback was removed in JAX > 0.9.1:

 I'm realizing the inheritance structure is worse than expected—`GroupedGemmPrimitive` doesn't implement any of the three critical methods itself, relying entirely on a base class that either returns `NotImplemented`, provides a default rule, or raises `NotImplementedError` for `infer_sharding_from_operands`.

The actual error is a `TypeError` about `NoneType` not being callable from the callback, which suggests the `infer_sharding_from_operands` callback is being set to `None` somewhere in the registration process. I'm suspecting this happens when `_JAX_GSPMD_SUPPORTED` is `False` (for JAX > 0.9.1)—the code only passes `partition` and `sharding_rule` to `def_partition`, leaving the callback unset, and then XLA tries to invoke it anyway, causing the crash.

But looking at the actual error message more carefully, when I re-enable Shardy, the real issue surfaces: the base class's abstract `shardy_sharding_rule` just returns `"... -> ..."` (a single-operand rule), but `GroupedGemmPrimitive` has 13 operands, so Shardy rejects it immediately. So the problem is that neither the GSPMD path (callback is `None`) nor the Shardy path (rule doesn't match the operation signature) actually works. I need to either implement a proper Shardy rule for this primitive, add the GSPMD methods back if that's still supported, or restructure the grouped GEMM to avoid custom partitioning altogether.

Option 3 is intriguing—wrapping the grouped GEMM inside a `shard_map` where inputs are already physically distributed. Inside `shard_map`, the body operates on local per-device shapes and the manual mesh axes are restricted, so maybe the custom partitioning rules wouldn't even be invoked or could just be no-ops. But I'm not entirely sure if that sidesteps the issue or just delays it. Let me check the JAX version first to understand what's actually supported, then decide which path to take.

I notice JAX isn't available in my current environment, but the user is working on a different machine with a path in their lustre home directory. I should check what's accessible in their workspace to see if there are any hints about which JAX version they're using.

Their installed JAX is > 0.9.1 (the GSPMD callback was removed). Let me confirm what they're hitting and check existing tests for clues:

 They're running JAX 0.9.1 or thereabouts, with the runtime located in `/opt/jax/jax/`. I'm looking at a comment in `base.py` that explains GSPMD sharding propagation was removed in newer JAX versions, so they're only registering it for older versions to maintain backwards compatibility.

The version check sets `_JAX_GSPMD_SUPPORTED = True` for JAX 0.9.1, which means it passes the GSPMD callback. But here's the puzzle — the error says `'NoneType' object is not callable`, yet `cls.infer_sharding_from_operands` is a classmethod that raises `NotImplementedError`, not `None`. So where's the `None` coming from?

Looking at the JAX traceback, the error originates in `/opt/jax/jax/_src/custom_partitioning.py` at line 214 inside `_custom_partitioning_infer_sharding_from_operands`. The function receives the `infer_sharding_from_operands` argument, and if JAX is newer than 0.9.1, something's going wrong with how it's being handled.

The real issue is that the user's JAX version is actually greater than 0.9.1, so `_JAX_GSPMD_SUPPORTED` evaluates to `False` in TE, meaning no GSPMD callback gets registered. But XLA's GSPMD pipeline still tries to invoke the callback because the lowered HLO has the `custom_partitioner` attribute set. Since the callback is `None`, XLA crashes with that `TypeError`. # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Reference: pure-JAX grouped GEMM (no TE primitive, no shard_map).
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Why not call ``grouped_dense`` here? See the long comment at the top of the
# file: a *standalone* (non-shard_map) call to TE's grouped GEMM crashes the
# partitioner because the primitive lacks a Shardy/GSPMD rule. For our POC the
# reference only needs to be mathematically correct; since we route the same
# number of tokens to every expert (synthetic uniform routing built by
# ``_make_uniform_grouped_inputs``), grouped_dense reduces to a single batched
# einsum that we can write in plain JAX.
#
# Mathematical equivalence: for ``group_sizes = [M/E] * E``, the M-axis of x
# is partitioned into E equal contiguous chunks of size M/E, with chunk e
# matmul'd against ``kernel[e] : (K, N)``. Reshaping x to (E, M/E, K) and
# kernel to (E, K, N), this is exactly ``einsum('esk,ekn->esn', x_g, kernel)``
# reshaped back to (M, N).

def grouped_dense_reference(x, kernel, group_sizes):
    """Pure-JAX equivalent of ``grouped_dense`` for uniform group_sizes.

    The ``group_sizes`` arg is accepted for API compatibility with the TE
    function but not consumed -- we trust the caller's uniform-routing
    invariant (every expert receives the same M/E tokens, in expert order).
    A debug assert below catches violations of that invariant.
    """
    del group_sizes  # uniform routing assumed
    M, K = x.shape
    E, K2, N = kernel.shape
    assert K == K2, f"K mismatch: x has {K}, kernel has {K2}"
    assert M % E == 0, (
        f"This reference assumes uniform routing (M divisible by E); "
        f"got M={M}, E={E}."
    )
    sz = M // E
    x_g = x.reshape(E, sz, K)
    y_g = jnp.einsum("esk,ekn->esn", x_g, kernel)
    return y_g.reshape(M, N)

### What changed and why

**Earlier diagnosis (was wrong): "disable Shardy and use GSPMD."**
That just traded the Shardy-side error for a GSPMD-side error because:

- TE's `cpp_extensions/base.py:26` only registers GSPMD callbacks when `jax.__version__ <= 0.9.1` (a comment there even says "GSPMD propagation removed in JAX > 0.9.1").
- Your `/opt/jax` install is > 0.9.1, so TE's `register_primitive` skipped the GSPMD callback for `GroupedGemmPrimitive` entirely.
- XLA's GSPMD propagation pipeline still wanted to call the (null) `infer_sharding_from_operands` callback → `'NoneType' object is not callable`.

**Actual root cause:** `GroupedGemmPrimitive` (`gemm.py:1427`) provides **neither** a `shardy_sharding_rule` nor (on JAX > 0.9.1) GSPMD callbacks. It is *only* usable when its lowering is short-circuited by being inside a `shard_map` body (where the mesh is "manual" and the partitioner has nothing to propagate). The single-tensor `GemmPrimitive` (line 1145) does have a Shardy rule; the grouped version simply doesn't, and there's no test in TE that calls it standalone.

**The two changes I made:**

1. `jax_poc_grouped_dense_fsdp.py` — removed the `JAX_USE_SHARDY_PARTITIONER=false` toggle (let Shardy stay on, the system default), and **rewrote `grouped_dense_reference` as a pure-JAX einsum**:

```249:286:jax_poc_grouped_dense_fsdp.py
def grouped_dense_reference(x, kernel, group_sizes):
    """Pure-JAX equivalent of ``grouped_dense`` for uniform group_sizes.
    ...
    """
    del group_sizes
    M, K = x.shape
    E, K2, N = kernel.shape
    assert M % E == 0
    sz = M // E
    x_g = x.reshape(E, sz, K)
    y_g = jnp.einsum("esk,ekn->esn", x_g, kernel)
    return y_g.reshape(M, N)
```

This is mathematically identical to `grouped_dense` for our uniform routing. The Variants A and B remain unchanged — they call the real `grouped_dense` inside a `shard_map`, just like `test_multi_process_distributed_grouped_gemm.py` does.

2. `jax_poc_fsdp_te_dense.py` — reverted the Shardy disable (it isn't needed; the single-tensor `dense` does have a proper Shardy rule).

### Implication for the upcoming MoEBlock work

This explains a real constraint on Experiment 1 ("quantize before FSDP all-gather"): `grouped_dense` **must always be called from inside a `shard_map`** (or at least from a context where partitioning is manual). The current MoEBlock satisfies this in `_forward_ring_ep`. Any future code path you add that wants to call `grouped_dense` with FSDP must keep that wrapping invariant (or first land a `shardy_sharding_rule` for `GroupedGemmPrimitive` upstream — that would be the proper long-term fix and is worth filing as a TE bug).

if hasattr(jax, "shard_map"):
    _shard_map_impl = jax.shard_map
else:
    from jax.experimental.shard_map import shard_map as _shard_map_impl  # type: ignore[no-redef]


def shard_map(f, *, mesh, in_specs, out_specs, check_vma: bool = True):
    """Version-agnostic shard_map wrapper.

    The ``check_vma`` flag (renamed from the older ``check_rep`` in recent JAX)
    enforces Varying-Manual-Axis (VMA) annotation propagation through the body
    of the shard_map. When a TE custom_call (e.g. ``tex.grouped_gemm``) appears
    inside the body, it doesn't propagate the ``{V:axis}`` annotation through
    its FFI boundary, so the bwd of ``grouped_dense`` produces a tensor without
    the annotation that the primal had -- which JAX's ``custom_vjp`` reports as
    "manual axis types do not match".
    Set ``check_vma=False`` whenever the body wraps such a custom_call. This is
    exactly what TE's own multi-process grouped-GEMM test does (passing
    ``check_rep=False`` -- same semantics, older name).
    """
    kwargs = dict(mesh=mesh, in_specs=in_specs, out_specs=out_specs)
    # Try the modern (check_vma) name first, then the legacy (check_rep) name,
    # then no flag (very old JAX). This keeps the script portable across the
    # 0.8 -> 0.9 -> 0.10 API churn.
    for kw in ("check_vma", "check_rep"):
        try:
            return _shard_map_impl(f, **kwargs, **{kw: check_vma})
        except TypeError:
            continue
    return _shard_map_impl(f, **kwargs)

        _ffn_fwd_shard_fn_B,

        check_vma=False,  # see ffn_block_A for rationale

File parses. Re-run `python3 jax_poc_grouped_dense_fsdp.py`.

### What this error was, in one paragraph

Inside a `shard_map`, JAX tags every per-device tensor with which manual mesh axes it varies along — your `x` has type `float32[128,32]{V:fsdp}` ("varies along fsdp"). When a `custom_vjp` is invoked inside that body, JAX checks that the bwd's outputs carry the same `{V:axis}` tags as the corresponding primal inputs. TE's `tex.grouped_gemm` is an opaque FFI custom_call — when the autodiff'd bwd of `grouped_dense` produces `dx = grouped_gemm(dy, kernel_T, …)`, the FFI boundary doesn't propagate the `{V:fsdp}` tag through. JAX then sees a mismatch (`float32[128,32]` vs `float32[128,32]{V:fsdp}`) and fails. This check is controlled by **`check_vma`** (the new name in your JAX for the `check_rep` flag in JAX ≤ 0.9). TE's own multi-process grouped-GEMM test sets `check_rep=False` for exactly this reason (`tests/jax/test_multi_process_distributed_grouped_gemm.py:53,97`).

### What I changed

1. **Made our `shard_map` wrapper version-agnostic for `check_vma`/`check_rep`** — it tries `check_vma` first (your JAX), then falls back to `check_rep` (older JAX), then to no flag. Default is `True` to match JAX's default.

```93:122:jax_poc_grouped_dense_fsdp.py
def shard_map(f, *, mesh, in_specs, out_specs, check_vma: bool = True):
    """Version-agnostic shard_map wrapper.
    …
    """
    kwargs = dict(mesh=mesh, in_specs=in_specs, out_specs=out_specs)
    for kw in ("check_vma", "check_rep"):
        try:
            return _shard_map_impl(f, **kwargs, **{kw: check_vma})
        except TypeError:
            continue
    return _shard_map_impl(f, **kwargs)
```

2. **Passed `check_vma=False` to all three `shard_map` calls** in Variant A, Variant B fwd, and Variant B bwd, with an inline comment explaining why.

3. **Pre-fixed a latent bug** in Variant B's bwd: it returned `None` for the `group_sizes` tangent, but `out_specs` requires every output to be a real array with matching per-shard shape. Replaced with `jnp.zeros_like(group_sizes_local)`. Without this fix, you would have hit a different error after clearing the current one.

### Implications for MoEBlock work

- The current `MoEBlock._forward_ring_ep` already uses `check_rep=False` (line 539), so it's protected from this exact failure mode. Good.
- **Any new `shard_map` we add around `grouped_dense` (e.g., a separate fwd/bwd shard_map for Experiment 1) must also use `check_vma=False`** — and we should keep an eye out for this when writing tests against newer JAX where `check_rep` no longer exists.
- The right long-term fix in TE is to make `tex.grouped_gemm` propagate the VMA annotation through its FFI lowering (so `check_vma=True` works), but that's a multi-line MLIR/lowering change well outside our POC scope.

Once you confirm POC #4 runs cleanly end-to-end, I can move on to drafting the `kernel_fsdp_info` plumbing change in `grouped_dense` for Experiment 1 (quantize-before-FSDP-AG).

import time as _time


def _stage(label):
    """Tiny progress helper so we can see where compile time goes.

    The very first jitted call inside a multi-device program triggers (a) full
    XLA compilation of the jaxpr (which for a custom_vjp + shard_map + TE
    custom_calls graph can easily be 30-60s) and (b) NCCL clique setup
    (another few seconds). JAX prints a "rendezvous waiting 10s" warning
    during NCCL setup; that is NOT a hang, just a heartbeat. Use this stage
    helper to confirm we're making progress between major steps.
    """
    print(f"[{_time.strftime('%H:%M:%S')}] {label}", flush=True)

Re-run and please be patient — let it sit for at least a few minutes after the warning. The new instrumentation will tell us:

### What you'll see

1. Timestamped `[HH:MM:SS]` stage markers between every major step.
2. The **bwd is now lowered + compiled separately, before being executed**, and we dump that HLO + collective summary *before* running it. If the lower-and-compile step itself succeeds, the bwd graph is structurally sound — the only thing left to fail is the NCCL execution.
3. `jax.block_until_ready(...)` is now called after each step so a stage marker means the result is actually materialized, not just dispatched.

### What the diagnostic will tell us

- **If you see `A bwd: compiled` and then the rendezvous warning** → the bwd HLO compiled fine, and we're stuck in NCCL setup. That's normal first-call latency; just wait. If it eventually completes, we're good.

- **If `A bwd: trace + lower` itself takes minutes and never reaches `compiled`** → it's an XLA compile-time problem (unlikely, but possible with custom_vjp + grouped GEMM).

- **If `A fwd: done` appears but `A bwd: trace + lower` hangs** → autodiff is stuck constructing the bwd jaxpr (possibly a real issue with `check_vma=False` interacting with TE's custom_vjp).

- **If `A bwd: run` is reached but never `A bwd: done` even after several minutes** → genuine NCCL deadlock, and we should look at the dumped `grouped_dense_variant_A_bwd_precompile.hlo.txt` to see if the bwd is missing a `psum-scatter` or has mismatched collectives between ranks.

### Why the warning is suspicious but not necessarily fatal

The line says `Acquire clique: devices=4:[0,1,2,3]; local_participants=4` — this is **NCCL's first-time clique setup** for a 4-device communicator inside a single process. On a system that hasn't issued a NCCL collective in this process before, this initialization can take a long time. **The forward pass also does an all_gather** (your `y_A` print succeeded), so NCCL was already partially initialized — but the bwd uses additional collective communicators (the autodiff-inserted `psum_scatter`), and creating a *new* clique for that can re-trigger the rendezvous.

### If it really is hung

If the new diagnostic shows `A bwd: run` reached but nothing follows for >2 min, the most likely cause is what I was suspicious of earlier: **`tex.grouped_gemm` strips the VMA annotation**, and combined with `check_vma=False`, JAX's autodiff may produce a jaxpr where the `psum_scatter` (bwd of `all_gather`) is elided as "not varying along fsdp" → ranks emit different collective sequences → deadlock. In that case, the dumped pre-run HLO will literally show `0` reduce-scatters (or a count that doesn't match the forward's `1` all-gather). That's the diagnostic to look for: **the bwd HLO must contain at least one `reduce-scatter` (or `all-reduce`) corresponding to the bwd of our forward `all_gather`**. If it doesn't, we'll switch to **Variant B (explicit fwd + bwd shard_maps with hand-written `psum_scatter`)** as the canonical path, which doesn't depend on autodiff seeing through the VMA-stripped grouped_gemm.

Run it again and paste the output — once we see exactly which stage stalls and what the pre-run bwd HLO contains, we'll know the next step.

input.new_string:
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Multi-process launcher
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# We support three launch styles:
#   1. mpirun / srun: OMPI_COMM_WORLD_RANK + OMPI_COMM_WORLD_SIZE OR SLURM_PROCID + SLURM_NTASKS
#   2. Manual:       JAX_POC_RANK, JAX_POC_NUM_PROCS, JAX_POC_COORD (e.g. host:port)
#   3. Single-proc:  none of the above set -> skip jax.distributed.initialize
#
# When in multi-process mode, this script binds each rank to a single GPU
# (CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=<rank>) so JAX sees exactly one device per process,
# matching what TE's tests assume.

def _init_distributed_or_single():
    """Return (is_multiproc, rank, num_procs). Initialise jax.distributed on demand."""
    rank = num_procs = None
    coord = None
    if "OMPI_COMM_WORLD_RANK" in os.environ and "OMPI_COMM_WORLD_SIZE" in os.environ:
        rank = int(os.environ["OMPI_COMM_WORLD_RANK"])
        num_procs = int(os.environ["OMPI_COMM_WORLD_SIZE"])
        coord = os.environ.get("JAX_POC_COORD")
    elif "SLURM_PROCID" in os.environ and "SLURM_NTASKS" in os.environ:
        rank = int(os.environ["SLURM_PROCID"])
        num_procs = int(os.environ["SLURM_NTASKS"])
        coord = os.environ.get("JAX_POC_COORD") or os.environ.get(
            "SLURM_LAUNCH_NODE_IPADDR", ""
        )
        if coord and ":" not in coord:
            coord = f"{coord}:12345"
    elif "JAX_POC_RANK" in os.environ and "JAX_POC_NUM_PROCS" in os.environ:
        rank = int(os.environ["JAX_POC_RANK"])
        num_procs = int(os.environ["JAX_POC_NUM_PROCS"])
        coord = os.environ.get("JAX_POC_COORD", "127.0.0.1:12345")
    else:
        return False, 0, 1

    # Bind each rank to one GPU BEFORE importing jax.distributed.
    # If user already set CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES we respect it.
    if "CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES" not in os.environ:
        os.environ["CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES"] = str(rank)

    if not coord:
        coord = "127.0.0.1:12345"

    print(f"[rank {rank}/{num_procs}] coord={coord} CVD={os.environ.get('CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES')}",
          flush=True)
    import jax as _jax_for_init  # noqa: F401  (already imported above)
    jax.distributed.initialize(
        coordinator_address=coord, num_processes=num_procs, process_id=rank
    )
    return True, rank, num_procs


_IS_MULTIPROC, _RANK, _NUM_PROCS = _init_distributed_or_single()


if hasattr(jax, "shard_map"):
    _shard_map_impl = jax.shard_map
else:
    from jax.experimental.shard_map import shard_map as _shard_map_impl  # type: ignore[no-redef]

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Multi-process launcher
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Recommended launch (4 GPUs):
#
#   mpirun -np 4 -x JAX_POC_COORD=127.0.0.1:12345 python jax_poc_grouped_dense_fsdp.py
#
# Or with srun:
#
#   srun -n 4 --gpus-per-task=1 \
#        --export=ALL,JAX_POC_COORD=$(hostname):12345 \
#        python jax_poc_grouped_dense_fsdp.py
#
# Or manually (in 4 separate shells):
#
#   JAX_POC_RANK=0 JAX_POC_NUM_PROCS=4 JAX_POC_COORD=127.0.0.1:12345 \
#       CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0 python jax_poc_grouped_dense_fsdp.py
#   ...same with rank=1..3, CVD=1..3...
#
# If none of the rank env vars are set, we fall back to single-process mode,
# which works only when the script avoids ``grouped_dense`` (e.g. for the
# pure-JAX einsum reference). With multi-GPU + grouped_dense in a single
# process, you will get "CUDA Error: invalid resource handle" from cuBLAS
# (see the long comment near the top of the file).

def _init_distributed_or_single():
    """Return (is_multiproc, rank, num_procs). Initialise jax.distributed on demand."""
    rank = num_procs = coord = None

    if "OMPI_COMM_WORLD_RANK" in os.environ and "OMPI_COMM_WORLD_SIZE" in os.environ:
        rank = int(os.environ["OMPI_COMM_WORLD_RANK"])
        num_procs = int(os.environ["OMPI_COMM_WORLD_SIZE"])
    elif "SLURM_PROCID" in os.environ and "SLURM_NTASKS" in os.environ:
        rank = int(os.environ["SLURM_PROCID"])
        num_procs = int(os.environ["SLURM_NTASKS"])
    elif "JAX_POC_RANK" in os.environ and "JAX_POC_NUM_PROCS" in os.environ:
        rank = int(os.environ["JAX_POC_RANK"])
        num_procs = int(os.environ["JAX_POC_NUM_PROCS"])
    else:
        return False, 0, 1

    coord = os.environ.get("JAX_POC_COORD") or "127.0.0.1:12345"

    # Bind each rank to one GPU. Respect a user-provided CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES
    # so that mpirun/srun-style local-rank wrappers still work; otherwise
    # default to CVD=<rank> which assumes ranks 0..N-1 map to GPUs 0..N-1.
    if "CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES" not in os.environ:
        os.environ["CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES"] = str(rank)

    print(
        f"[rank {rank}/{num_procs}] coord={coord} "
        f"CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES={os.environ.get('CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES')}",
        flush=True,
    )
    jax.distributed.initialize(
        coordinator_address=coord, num_processes=num_procs, process_id=rank
    )
    return True, rank, num_procs


_IS_MULTIPROC, _RANK, _NUM_PROCS = _init_distributed_or_single()


def _is_rank0() -> bool:
    return _RANK == 0


def _rprint(*a, **kw):
    """Print only from rank 0 (or always, in single-proc mode)."""
    if _is_rank0():
        print(*a, **kw)

def _stage(label):
    """Tiny progress helper so we can see where compile time goes.

    The very first jitted call inside a multi-device program triggers (a) full
    XLA compilation of the jaxpr (which for a custom_vjp + shard_map + TE
    custom_calls graph can easily be 30-60s) and (b) NCCL clique setup
    (another few seconds). JAX prints a "rendezvous waiting 10s" warning
    during NCCL setup; that is NOT a hang, just a heartbeat.
    """
    if _is_rank0():
        print(f"[{_time.strftime('%H:%M:%S')}] {label}", flush=True)


def main():
    jax.config.update("jax_default_matmul_precision", "highest")

    if not _IS_MULTIPROC and len(DEVICES) > 1:
        raise SystemExit(
            "ERROR: this script must be launched as one process per GPU.\n"
            "TE's grouped_gemm caches CUDA streams in a process-static array;\n"
            "those streams are bound to whichever device was current at first\n"
            "use, so a single multi-GPU process gets 'CUDA: invalid resource\n"
            "handle' as soon as the kernel runs on any other device.\n\n"
            "Launch with one of:\n"
            "  mpirun -np N -x JAX_POC_COORD=127.0.0.1:12345 \\\n"
            "      python jax_poc_grouped_dense_fsdp.py\n"
            "  srun -n N --gpus-per-task=1 \\\n"
            "      --export=ALL,JAX_POC_COORD=$(hostname):12345 \\\n"
            "      python jax_poc_grouped_dense_fsdp.py\n"
            "  # or set JAX_POC_RANK / JAX_POC_NUM_PROCS / JAX_POC_COORD by hand"
        )

    _rprint(f"JAX devices ({len(DEVICES)}): {[str(d) for d in DEVICES]}")
    _rprint(f"Mesh: {MESH}\n")
